questions.question:
Consider the synchronous sequential circuit shown below. The circuit has three D flip-flops (FF1, FF2, FF3) with outputs Q1, Q2, Q3. FF1 has an XNOR gate at its input fed by the Q-bar outputs of FF1 and FF3. The connections are: D2 = Q1, D3 = Q2 (shift-register chain), and D1 = XNOR(Q1_bar, Q3_bar).

The states are named by (Q1 Q2 Q3) as follows:
Q1=0,Q2=0,Q3=0 -> S0
Q1=0,Q2=0,Q3=1 -> S1
...
Q1=1,Q2=1,Q3=1 -> S7

Given that the initial state of the circuit is S4 (Q1=1, Q2=0, Q3=0), identify the set of states which are NOT reachable.
questions.to_find: The set of states that are not reachable when the circuit starts in state S4.

understand.plain: We have a 3-bit synchronous sequential circuit (a feedback shift register) with 8 possible states S0 to S7. Starting from S4, we trace all transitions to find which states are visited. Any state never visited in any reachable sequence is 'not reachable'. The key is to build the state-transition table and follow the path from S4.

keywords.explain: A digital circuit where flip-flops change state only on the clock edge; the next state is determined by the current state and inputs.
keywords.example: A 3-bit counter updates all three flip-flops simultaneously on every clock pulse.

keywords.explain: Outputs 1 when both inputs are equal (both 0 or both 1); it is the complement of XOR.
A state that can be reached from the initial state by following zero or more transitions.
keywords.example: If S4->S2->S5, then S2 and S5 are reachable from S4.

class='tag ink'>S2</text><circle cx='350' cy='160' r='24' class='bg'/><text x='350' y='166' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>S5</text><circle cx='80' cy='110' r='24' class='bg'/><text x='80' cx='260' cy='210' r='24' class='no'/><text x='260' y='216' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>S7</text><text x='260' y='248' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>S7 is unreachable (self-loop, never entered from S4 chain)</text><line x1='260' y1='134' x2='200' y2='145' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#arr)'/><line x1='194' y1='145' x2='360' y2='152' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#arr)'/><line x1='374' y1='152' x2='452' y2='122' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#arr)'/><line x1='440' y1='134' x2='452' y2='178' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#arr)'/><line x1='428' y1='200' x2='116' y2='200' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#arr)'/><line x1='80' y1='176' x2='80' y2='134' stroke='#7F77DD' refX='6' refY='3' orient='auto'><path d='M0,0 L0,6 L8,3 z' fill='#7F77DD'/></marker></defs></svg>
understand.visual_alt: State transition cycle: S4 -> S2 -> S5 -> S6 -> S3 -> S1 -> S0 -> S4 (7-state cycle). S7 is a self-loop and never entered from the main cycle, making it unreachable.

given.aim: Find which states among S0-S7 can never be reached starting from initial state S4.

terms.meaning: The next value of Q1 is the XNOR of the complemented outputs of FF1 and FF3. Since XNOR(A_bar, B_bar) = XNOR(A, B), this equals Q1 XNOR Q3.
terms.connects: Provides the feedback that determines the sequence of states.

terms.meaning: FF2 and FF3 simply shift the current Q1 and Q2 values respectively on each clock.
terms.example: If Q1=1, Q2=0: next Q2=1, next Q3=0.
terms.connects: Makes the circuit a feedback shift register.

terms.meaning: The starting point from which reachability is determined.
terms.example: All states reachable from S4 in any number of steps are reachable states.
terms.connects: Determines which cycle of the state graph we start in.

given.plan: Build the complete state-transition table using D1 = Q1 XNOR Q3, D2 = Q1, D3 = Q2. Then trace all states reachable from S4. Any state not in the reachable set is the answer.

steps.title: Build state-transition table
steps.note: Note: XNOR(A_bar, B_bar) = XNOR(A,B) because complementing both inputs of XNOR leaves the output unchanged.

steps.title: Trace reachable states from S4
steps.note: S7 forms its own isolated self-loop. Since no other state transitions into S7, it is permanently unreachable from S4.

solution.result: The state S7 (Q1=1, Q2=1, Q3=1) is the only state not reachable from initial state S4. The circuit cycles through all other 7 states: S4 -> S2 -> S5 -> S6 -> S3 -> S1 -> S0 -> S4.

questions.formula_note: Feedback shift register state transition analysis: compute Next(Q1,Q2,Q3) = (Q1 XNOR Q3, Q1, Q2) and trace reachable states from the given initial state.

questions.question:
Consider a logic circuit shown in figure below. The functions f1, f2 and f (in canonical sum of products form in decimal notation) are:

f1(w,x,y,z) = sum(8,9,10)
f2(w,x,y,z) = sum(7,8,12,13,14,15)
f(w,x,y,z)  = sum(8,9)

The circuit has f1 and f2 as inputs to an AND gate, and f3 as a second input to an OR gate whose output is f. That is: f = (f1 AND f2) OR f3.

The function f3 is

A. sum(9,10)
B. sum(9)
C. sum(1,8,9)
D. sum(8,10,15)
questions.to_find: The canonical SOP expression for f3 such that f = (f1 AND f2) OR f3 = sum(8,9).

understand.plain: The logic circuit computes f as the OR of two signals: (f1 AND f2) and f3. Since we know f1, f2, and f, we can work backwards to determine what f3 must be. The AND of two SOP functions gives only the minterms common to both; then OR with f3 must produce exactly the minterms of f.

keywords.explain: A Boolean function expressed as the OR of all minterms (standard product terms) where the function equals 1. Each minterm corresponds to one row of the truth table.
keywords.example: f = sum(0,3) means f=1 when (w,x,y,z)=0000 or 0011.

keywords.explain: The AND of two SOP functions is 1 only on minterms that are present in BOTH functions (set intersection).
The OR of two SOP functions is 1 on all minterms present in EITHER function (set union).

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='420' height='180'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='0' y='0' width='420' height='180' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='30' y='55' class='lbl ink'>f1</text><text x='30' y='95' class='lbl ink'>f2</text><rect x='80' y='40' width='70' height='60' rx='6' class='hi'/><text x='115' y='76' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>AND</text><line x1='55' y1='55' x2='80' y2='60' stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='55' y1='95' x2='80' y2='80' stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='150' y1='70' x2='210' y2='70' stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='2'/><text x='30' y='135' class='lbl ink'>f3</text><rect x='210' y='50' width='70' height='80' rx='6' class='hi'/><text x='245' y='96' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>OR</text><line x1='55' y1='135' x2='210' y2='110' stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='280' y1='90' x2='340' y2='90' stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='2'/><text x='355' y='95' class='lbl ink'>f</text><text x='90' y='130' class='sub mut'>f1 AND f2 = {8}</text><text x='90' y='150' class='sub mut'>f3 must add {9} only</text><text x='200' y='170' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>f = {8} union {9} = {8,9} ✓</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Circuit diagram: f1 and f2 feed an AND gate whose output carries minterm {8} (the intersection). That AND result and f3 feed an OR gate producing f = sum(8,9). So f3 must supply exactly minterm {9}.

given.aim: Find f3 given that f = (f1 AND f2) OR f3, where f1 = sum(8,9,10), f2 = sum(7,8,12,13,14,15), f = sum(8,9).

terms.meaning: f1 is 1 for minterms 8, 9, and 10 (w=1,x=0,y=0,z=0/1 and w=1,x=0,y=1,z=0).
terms.connects: Used to compute f1 AND f2 by finding common minterms with f2.

terms.example: Minterm 8 = 1000 is in both f1 and f2.
terms.connects: The intersection with f1 determines the first term going into the OR gate.

terms.meaning: The final output f must equal 1 only for minterms 8 and 9.
terms.connects: Knowing f and (f1 AND f2) lets us back-calculate f3 = f minus (f1 AND f2).

given.plan: Step 1: Compute f1 AND f2 = intersection of their minterm sets. Step 2: Since f = (f1 AND f2) OR f3, solve for f3: f3 must be a subset of f that, when unioned with (f1 AND f2), gives exactly f. Specifically, f3 = f - (f1 AND f2) at minimum, and any minterms of f3 outside f would violate f = sum(8,9).

steps.title: Compute f1 AND f2 (intersection of minterm sets)
steps.formula_raw: f1 AND f2 = {minterms in both f1 and f2}
steps.note: Only minterm 8 (binary 1000) appears in both f1 and f2.

steps.title: Solve for f3 using f = (f1 AND f2) OR f3
steps.formula_raw: f = A OR f3  =>  f3 subset must satisfy: A union f3 = f
steps.apply:
We need: sum(8) OR f3 = sum(8,9)
f3 must contain minterm 9 (to add it to the output).
f3 must NOT contain any minterm outside {8,9}, otherwise f would have extra minterms.
Minimal solution: f3 = sum(9).
Check option A: f3=sum(9,10) -> sum(8) OR sum(9,10) = sum(8,9,10) != sum(8,9). FAIL.
Check option B: f3=sum(9) -> sum(8) OR sum(9) = sum(8,9) = f. PASS.
Check option C: f3=sum(1,8,9) -> sum(8) OR sum(1,8,9) = sum(1,8,9) != sum(8,9). FAIL.
Check option D: f3=sum(8,10,15) -> sum(8) OR sum(8,10,15) = sum(8,10,15) != sum(8,9). FAIL.
steps.note: The key insight: f3 can include minterm 8 without harm (since it is already in the output), but must add 9 and nothing else not already in f.

questions.formula_note: Boolean algebra on canonical SOP forms: AND of two SOP functions = set intersection of minterms; OR of two SOP functions = set union of minterms. Back-solve f3 from f = (f1 AND f2) OR f3.
